Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'm 27, working part-time, and just trying to get through this degree at this point. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, maybe on a weekend or a slow evening, and then try to catch up on whatever I'd missed. It wasn't organized at all—more like damage control when things piled up.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. It completely depended on my work schedule and whether I had deadlines breathing down my neck. Some weeks I'd do nothing, then suddenly I'd cram in a bunch of hours when an assignment was due. Regular study times sound nice in theory, but my life doesn't really allow for that kind of structure.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. Like, I'd realize I hadn't checked the course in a week and wonder what I'd missed. Sometimes a classmate would message me about something due, and that would push me to actually open the platform. It was rarely because I was excited to learn something new.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more often, especially after the peer support stuff started and people were actually reaching out. But it always felt like an obligation rather than something natural. I never got into a rhythm with it.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things or during my commute. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too, especially when other students explained things in simpler terms than the instructor. Those peer interactions saved me a few times.
Q6: I ignored most of the reading materials and the optional quizzes. Reading long PDFs on a screen just doesn't work for me—I zone out completely. The quizzes felt pointless if they weren't graded, so I'd tell myself I'd do them later and then never did. I know I probably should have engaged more, but I just couldn't bring myself to care.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me pass an assignment or the exam. That's pretty much it. If I couldn't see an immediate connection to something that would affect my grade, I'd skip it. I know that sounds bad, but when you're juggling everything I'm juggling, you have to prioritize ruthlessly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones at least had some urgency attached to them. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that might be nice to do someday' and then never touch it. The lack of consequences made it easy to deprioritize them indefinitely.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near a computer. I also used some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than our course materials. None of that would show up in the system, but it was often how I actually learned the stuff I needed for exams.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from downloaded files or my own notes without being logged in at all. I'd sometimes listen to lectures while working my job, just absorbing information passively. The LMS would show I was inactive, but I was actually processing course content in my own way.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it multiple times over the following days without ever logging back in. Same with any PDFs I actually bothered to save—I'd read them on my phone during breaks at work. One download could turn into hours of study that the system never saw.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough. I was picking up extra shifts at work because I needed the money, and I just couldn't keep up. Everything felt pointless and boring, like I was going through the motions without actually caring. The peer support helped a bit, but honestly, I was mostly just trying to survive.
Q13: Absolutely. When work got demanding, the LMS was the first thing to go. I'd be too tired to focus on anything after a long shift. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them entirely sometimes—if I didn't understand what was expected, I'd just put it off until the last minute or skip it altogether.
Q14: All the time. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills, work drama, whatever. Physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. That happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially the bigger assignments. I knew they were important, but the thought of starting them felt overwhelming. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. It's not that I didn't care—I just couldn't summon the energy to actually begin.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was a day or two away, then scramble to get something done. The LMS became a lot more interesting when there was a countdown involved. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If it was positive, I'd feel briefly okay about myself. If it was negative, I'd feel bad for a minute and then push it out of my mind. It never really motivated me to engage more with future work.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, which meant it was easy to fall behind and hard to catch up. Everything built on everything else, so when I missed stuff early on, I felt lost later. The weekly modules were clear enough, but they didn't really accommodate people like me who can't keep a consistent schedule.
Q19: The flexibility of having recorded lectures helped a lot—I could access them whenever. But the amount of reading required was a barrier for me as an auditory learner. More audio content or podcasts would have made engagement easier. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made them feel like a chore.
Q20: I think some people might be genuinely trying to figure out what's going on but struggling to produce actual work. Like, they're clicking around, watching videos, reading stuff, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or don't understand what's expected. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My exam score and assignment scores were decent enough to pass, but my LMS activity was pretty low. I did a lot of studying offline, listening to recordings and reviewing notes on my own. The system doesn't capture that kind of engagement, so it looks like I wasn't trying when I actually was.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or cared about it. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They're a very incomplete picture of what learning actually looks like.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and wanting to understand. I haven't felt truly engaged in a while, honestly. I'm mostly just trying to get the credential.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how burned out I am, or how much I'm juggling outside of school. It wouldn't capture the peer conversations that actually helped me understand things, or the hours I spent listening to lectures during my commute. It definitely wouldn't show that I'm bored and just going through the motions most of the time.
